We should often consider the one who suffered here, and ask why, as well as what he suffered, so that by meditating on this, we may be more humbled for sin and more convinced of our danger, so that we may feel more gratitude and love, compelling us to live to His glory who died for our salvation. From this we learn, when in affliction, to commit the keeping of our souls to God, so that we are not made bitter with discontent or sink into despair. David was hated wrongfully, but the words apply even more fully to Christ. In a world where unrighteousness is so prevalent, we must not be surprised if we meet those who are our enemies wrongfully. Let us take care that we never do wrong; then if we receive wrong, we may better bear it. By the satisfaction Christ made to God for our sin by his blood, he restored what he did not take away; he paid our debt and suffered for our offenses. Even when we can plead “Not guilty” regarding men’s unjust accusations, yet before God we must acknowledge ourselves to deserve all that is brought upon us. All our sins arise from our foolishness. They are all done in God's sight. David complains of the unkindness of friends and relatives. This was fulfilled in Christ, whose brothers did not believe on him, and who was forsaken by his disciples. Christ made satisfaction for us, not only by setting aside the honors due to God, but by submitting to the greatest dishonors that could be done to any man. We need not be discouraged if our zeal for the truths, precepts, and worship of God provokes some and causes others to mock our godly sorrow and deadness to the world.

Whatever deep waters of affliction or temptation we sink into, whatever floods of trouble or ungodly people seem ready to overwhelm us, let us persevere in prayer to our Lord to save us. The signs of God’s favor to us are enough to keep our spirits from sinking in the deepest outward troubles. If we think well of God and continue to do so under the greatest hardships, we need not fear but he will do well for us. And if at any time we are called on to suffer reproach and shame for Christ's sake, this may be our comfort, that he knows it. It is hard for one who values a good name to be oppressed with a bad one; but when we consider what a favor it is to be accounted worthy to suffer shame for the name of Jesus, we shall see that there is no reason why it should be heart-breaking to us. The sufferings of Christ were here particularly foretold, which proves the Scripture to be the word of God; and how exactly these predictions were fulfilled in Jesus Christ, which proves him to be the true Messiah. The vinegar and the gall given to him were a faint emblem of that bitter cup which he drank up, that we might drink the cup of salvation. We cannot expect too little from people; they are all miserable comforters; nor can we expect too much from the God of all comfort and consolation.

These are prophecies of the destruction of Christ's persecutors. Verses #(, ) are applied to the judgments of God upon the unbelieving Jews, in . When the supports of life and delights of sense, through the corruption of our nature, are made the food and fuel of sin, then our table is a snare. Their sin was that they would not see, but shut their eyes against the light, loving darkness rather; their punishment was that they should not see, but should be given up to their own hearts’ lusts which hardened them. Whoever rejects God's great salvation offered to them may justly fear that his indignation will be poured out upon them. If people will sin, the Lord will reckon for it. But those who have multiplied in sin may yet find mercy through the righteousness of the Mediator. God does not shut anyone out from that righteousness; the gospel excludes none who do not, by unbelief, shut themselves out. But those who are proud and self-willed, so that they will not come into God's righteousness, shall have their doom accordingly; they decide it themselves. Let those not expect any benefit thereby who are not glad to be beholden to it. It is better to be poor and sorrowful with the blessing of the Lord than rich and jovial under his curse. This may be applied to Christ. He was, when on earth, a man of sorrows that had not where to lay his head; but God exalted him. Let us call upon the Lord, and though poor and sorrowful, guilty and defiled, his salvation will set us up on high.

The psalmist concludes the psalm with holy joy and praise, which he began with complaints of his grief. It is a great comfort to us that humble and thankful praises are more pleasing to God than the most costly, pompous sacrifices. The humble will look to him and be glad; those who seek him through Christ will live and be comforted. God will do great things for the gospel church, in which let all who wish well to it rejoice. A seed will serve him on earth, and his servants will inherit his heavenly kingdom. Those that love his name will dwell before him forever. He who did not spare his own Son, but delivered him up for us all, how will he not also freely give us all things? Arise, you great Restorer of the ancient places to dwell in, and turn away ungodliness from your people.
